text.title: spatial-ui-patterns Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for spatial-ui-patterns. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Assign every panel exactly one anchor type before prototyping; do not defer anchoring to implementation.
rule.rationale: Anchor type determines interaction model, platform API, and ergonomic constraints. Changing it late requires redesigning transitions and input mappings.

rule.statement: World-locked panels must remain fixed in physical space; use for reference content that the user returns to across movement.
rule.rationale: World-locked panels feel grounded and reduce cognitive load for information the user consults repeatedly (maps, documentation, dashboards).

rule.statement: Head-locked panels must be reserved for transient notifications only; never use head-locked for primary navigation.
rule.rationale: Head-locked content follows gaze constantly, causing visual fatigue and disorientation within minutes. Transient use (under 5 seconds) is the only safe pattern.

rule.statement: Body-locked panels are the default for menus and tools used while moving; anchor to torso forward direction, not head orientation.
rule.rationale: Body-locked UI stays accessible without constant arm raising and does not drift with head turns, making it comfortable for extended use.

rule.statement: Hand-attached UI must cover no more than two high-frequency actions (e.g., mode toggle, undo); do not build a full toolbar on the hand.
rule.rationale: A hand that becomes a toolbar is always occupied. More than two actions causes the user to search the hand surface, breaking flow.

rule.statement: Place world-locked and body-locked panels at 0.5-2 m distance and within 30° eccentricity from forward gaze.
rule.rationale: Outside this envelope, users must rotate their head or lean, causing fatigue. The 30° eccentricity limit aligns with comfortable reading range without head movement.

rule.statement: Set the minimum touch target size to 4 cm in spatial UI; smaller targets are unreachable with reliable pinch or ray accuracy.
rule.rationale: Unlike touchscreen where a fingertip anchors the touch, spatial interaction uses ray casting or hand tracking with inherent jitter; 4 cm is the empirical floor.

rule.statement: Hand-attached panels must be placed 0.25-0.5 m from the user's eye point; outside this range hand tracking loses precision.
rule.rationale: Hand tracking cameras have a working volume; panels outside it produce tracking dropout and unreachable targets.

rule.statement: When targeting multiple XR platforms, define anchoring in platform-agnostic terms (world/head/body/hand) and map to platform APIs separately.
rule.rationale: Platform-specific anchor names differ enough to cause translation errors if mixed in design documents.

rule.statement: Support at minimum: snap to surfaces, group related windows, minimize to dock, and spatial memory (restore to last position).
rule.rationale: Users who place windows deliberately form spatial memory; losing positions on app restart breaks the mental model and forces repetitive layout work.

rule.statement: Persist spatial memory per-user across sessions, not per-app-launch; store anchor positions in platform world-anchor APIs.
rule.rationale: Per-launch spatial memory resets positions on every cold start, eliminating the ergonomic benefit of allowing users to arrange their workspace.

rule.statement: Provide a "recenter" gesture or voice command that returns body-locked UI to the comfortable default position.
rule.rationale: Users physically drift during extended sessions. Body-locked content that started centered drifts relative to the user's new forward direction; recenter corrects this without requiring the user to close and reopen panels.

rule.statement: Animate panel summon and dismiss with a spatial origin anchor (from the body or hand that invoked it); do not use instant pop-in.
rule.rationale: Instant appearance without a spatial origin disorients users — the panel seems to appear from nowhere. Animating from a source provides spatial context.

rule.statement: Consult platform manifests for window count and GPU memory limits before allowing users to open more than 3 simultaneous panels.
rule.rationale: Quest and Android XR impose OS-level limits on concurrent window count and GPU memory; exceeding them causes crashes or degraded rendering that the app cannot recover from gracefully.

rule.statement: Group logically related windows (e.g., inspector + canvas) so they can be moved and dismissed together.
rule.rationale: Independent movement of tightly coupled panels breaks layout coherence and forces users to reorganize relationships repeatedly.

rule.statement: Use depth as a secondary hierarchy signal after color and typography, not as a primary one.
rule.rationale: Many users have reduced stereo acuity (including a significant fraction with amblyopia or monocular vision); depth-first hierarchy excludes them and fails gracefully only if color/typography carry the signal too.

rule.statement: Test panel placement with at least 5 users including short and tall participants; ergonomic defaults assume a 50th-percentile adult standing.
rule.rationale: A 160 cm user and a 195 cm user experience very different comfort envelopes for the same world-locked panel. Seated users have additional constraints on forward reach and upward gaze.

rule.statement: Always verify input modality availability before proposing gaze+pinch, voice, or hand-tracking combinations.
rule.rationale: Voice and gaze are hardware-gated on some Quest variants; controller-only configurations on HoloLens change the entire interaction model. Proposing unsupported modalities produces a build that fails on the target device.

rule.statement: Pin SDK versions explicitly in every prompt when generating visionOS or Quest code; require the agent to cite API references.
rule.rationale: LLMs frequently emit deprecated APIs (e.g., OVRPlugin calls renamed in 2024+). Without version pinning, generated code compiles against documentation, not the installed SDK.

rule.statement: Declare coordinate system assumptions explicitly in every code generation prompt: visionOS (Y-up, right-handed, meters), Unity (Y-up, left-handed, meters), Unreal (Z-up, cm).
rule.rationale: Converting at the wrong layer breaks panel placement in ways that are not obvious until runtime; agents must not assume a coordinate system.

rule.statement: Require USDZ/USD scene mutations to be validated via usdcat before accepting agent output.
rule.rationale: USD authoring is finicky; agents produce syntactically plausible but semantically broken scenes that fail to load silently.

rule.statement: Flag any spatial UI specification that does not address vestibular sensitivity; provide a reduced-motion mode for all animated panel transitions.
rule.rationale: Spatial animations (panel summon, recenter, fly-in) trigger vestibular discomfort in users with motion sensitivity, a common condition that is easy to mitigate with a preference toggle.
